title: Environmental Monitoring Excursion in Grade A Fill Suite

preFill.initialObservation: Particle count exceeded the Grade A alert threshold for 12 minutes during routine environmental monitoring on shift A.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 18 May 2026 at 08:15 (Shift A), continuous environmental monitoring in the Grade A Fill Suite (Vaccine Filling Line 2) recorded ≥0.5 µm particle counts above the Grade A alert limit for 12 minutes during active vaccine filling. The excursion was flagged by the EM system at counter HEPA-FF-L2-07 and confirmed against the SOP-EM-014 trend.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Potential Major GMP impact. The excursion occurred in a classified Grade A aseptic zone during active vaccine filling, so sterility assurance for the units filled inside the exposure window must be assessed. No sterility failure is confirmed yet, which keeps this Major rather than Critical pending the batch impact review.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: Vaccine Filling Line 2 was placed on immediate engineering hold and batches VAC-26-0518-A and VAC-26-0518-B were quarantined pending QA disposition. Environmental re-test was initiated and HEPA-FF-L2-07 was flagged for integrity inspection before the suite returns to routine operation.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Grade A Fill Suite, Vaccine Filling Line 2, HEPA filter HEPA-FF-L2-07. Product impact is limited to vaccine batches VAC-26-0518-A and VAC-26-0518-B filled during Shift A. Source records: EM trend report (SOP-EM-014) and the filling-line event log.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm via a HEPA integrity / DOP test on HEPA-FF-L2-07, a review of the SOP-PM-HEPA-001 preventive-maintenance interval and filter age, the EM trend history for repeat alerts, and the SOP-FF-009 line event log to correlate the excursion with filling activity.

gateAnswers.question: How will effectiveness be verified?
gateAnswers.answer: Effectiveness is verified when post-remediation EM monitoring shows no Grade A alert or action excursions across at least three consecutive filling campaigns, the requalified HEPA passes integrity testing, and the revised maintenance interval is reflected in the planner with no recurrence over 90 days.

factors.rationale: The excursion occurred in a critical aseptic processing area.
The exposure was time-bound and can be assessed against batch records.
Potential batch impact requires QA assessment.

impact.rationale: Nova detected a Major deviation because the event occurred in a controlled filling area, involved a vaccine batch, and required immediate containment. The case is suitable for a structured 8D CAPA workflow.

disposisi.rationale: Excursion in a critical aseptic filling area involving active vaccine batches warrants full 8D investigation. Major severity confirmed. Assigned to Fill-Finish for structured corrective action workflow.

title: GMP Documentation Gap in Material Transfer Records

preFill.findingDescription: Material transfer records for three lots were completed after shift handover and lacked documented second-person verification.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: During internal audit IA-2026-QA-042 on 14 May 2026, QA Compliance found that warehouse material transfer records for three lots were completed after shift handover and lacked documented second-person verification, contrary to SOP-WH-021 and SOP-DOC-004.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Major GMP and data-integrity impact. Contemporaneous recording and independent verification underpin record reliability under GMP Chapter 4 and the BPOM 2023 Data Integrity guidance; the gap weakens audit defensibility for the affected transfers even though no material mix-up has been identified.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: The three affected material transfer records were segregated for a QA data-integrity review, and a temporary second-person verification check was reinstated at shift handover while the records are reconciled against physical stock.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Warehouse material transfer documentation for three lots recorded across the shift-handover window. Governing records: SOP-WH-021 (material transfer) and SOP-DOC-004 (documentation practices); finding logged in Q100+ as AUD-2026-0089.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm by reconciling the three transfer records against physical stock and ERP movements, interviewing the shift-handover personnel to map where verification ownership was lost, and reviewing whether SOP-WH-021 explicitly assigns real-time second-person verification.

gateAnswers.question: How will effectiveness be verified?
gateAnswers.answer: Effective when a follow-up record review over the next two months shows 100% contemporaneous completion with documented second-person verification, and the revised SOP plus retraining are confirmed in place with zero repeat observations at the next internal audit.

factors.rationale: Incomplete verification can weaken record reliability.
The issue appears limited and can be reconciled.
The issue may become an external audit observation if repeated.

impact.rationale: Nova classified this audit finding as Major because it involves GMP documentation control and potential data integrity risk across repeated material transfer records.

novaEntries: SOP-WH-021 did not explicitly assign real-time second-person verification ownership for material transfers.
userEntries: Verification was deferred to an end-of-shift reconciliation step instead of being contemporaneous.

novaEntries: Shift handover transferred record completion without confirming who owned the verification step.
No in-process check flagged records completed after the handover window.

confirmedRootCauses: Second-person verification ownership for material transfer records was not assigned across the shift-handover window, so records were completed retrospectively without independent verification.

verification.result: Two-month follow-up review of material transfer records confirmed 100% contemporaneous completion with documented second-person verification across all sampled transfers.

approvals.notes: Records reconciled and second-person verification reinstated. Endorsed for QA sign-off.

disposisi.rationale: Documentation gaps spanning multiple material transfer records indicate systemic non-compliance risk. Major severity confirmed per internal audit classification criteria. 8D workflow assigned to QA Compliance.

preFill.description: Hospital pharmacist observed visible particulate matter in one returned vial before administration. The vial remained sealed and was quarantined by the customer.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 12 May 2026, RS Hasan Sadikin Bandung reported that a hospital pharmacist observed visible particulate matter in one returned Vaccine Multi-dose Vial (lot VAC-26-0419-C) prior to administration. The vial remained sealed and was quarantined by the customer.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Major product-quality impact. Visible particulate in a parenteral vaccine is a patient-facing quality defect raised by a hospital customer; until the returned vial and retained samples are assessed, batch-wide impact cannot be excluded, though current scope is bounded to a single reported vial.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: The complaint vial is being recovered under a controlled return and retained samples for lot VAC-26-0419-C have been placed on QA review hold. Remaining distributed stock of the lot is monitored for further complaints pending the visual-inspection assessment.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Vaccine Multi-dose Vial, lot VAC-26-0419-C (expiry 30 Apr 2027), a single reported vial from RS Hasan Sadikin Bandung. Source records: customer photo, complaint intake form, and the batch visual-inspection reconciliation logged in Bizzmine as CMP-2026-0112.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm by assessing the returned vial and retained samples for particulate type (visual and microscopy), reviewing the lot's visual-inspection reconciliation and reject trend for borderline observations, and tracing whether any in-line inspection variance was escalated.

gateAnswers.question: How will effectiveness be verified?
gateAnswers.answer: Effective when the retained-sample assessment defines the particulate source, the strengthened visual-inspection reconciliation and escalation criteria are implemented, and no recurrence of particulate complaints is seen for the product over the following two production campaigns.

factors.rationale: The issue was detected by a customer before administration.
Particulate matter requires product quality and batch impact assessment.
The initial scope is limited but must be verified.

impact.rationale: Nova classified this complaint as Major because it involves visible particulate matter in a distributed vaccine product and requires batch record review, retained sample assessment, and complaint investigation.

nodes.question: Was particulate present in retained samples?
Review inspection reconciliation for the lot family.
nodes.conclusion: Inspection reconciliation escalation may be insufficient.

nodes.question: Assess distribution and customer handling evidence.
nodes.conclusion: External handling remains possible but less likely.

confirmedRootCauses: Visual inspection rejection trend was not escalated after repeated borderline observations in the same lot family.

verification.result: Returned-vial and retained-sample assessment identified the particulate source; two subsequent production campaigns of the product showed no recurrence of particulate complaints.

approvals.notes: Retained-sample assessment and strengthened inspection escalation reviewed. Endorsed for QA sign-off.

disposisi.rationale: Visible particulate matter in a vaccine vial constitutes a critical external complaint requiring batch traceability and root cause investigation. Major severity confirmed. Full 8D CAPA workflow required.

preFill.initialObservation: Operator recorded the wrong line number (Line 3 instead of Line 2) on the daily cleaning logbook entry; the cleaning itself was performed and verified on the correct line.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 20 May 2026 at 07:30 (Shift A), the operator completing the Solution Prep Room daily cleaning logbook recorded the wrong line identifier — Line 3 instead of Line 2 — for the morning cleaning record. The transposition was noticed during the same-shift supervisor review.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: No GMP or product impact. The cleaning was performed and independently verified on the correct line (Line 2); only the written line identifier was transposed. The entry was corrected the same shift with a single-line GMP correction (struck through, initialed, dated) per SOP-DOC-002, preserving the original entry.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: The entry was corrected contemporaneously with a single-line correction, initialed and dated by the operator and countersigned by the shift supervisor. No batch was affected and no further containment was required.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Single handwritten logbook entry — Solution Prep Room daily cleaning record, Shift A, 20 May 2026. No product, batch, lot, or equipment qualification affected. Governing record SOP-DOC-002 (good documentation practice).

factors.rationale: A handwritten identifier transposition with no effect on the cleaning performed.
Contemporaneous self-correction preserves the original entry and audit trail.
No batch, lot, or equipment qualification was affected.

impact.rationale: Minor: an isolated good-documentation-practice slip on a cleaning logbook, corrected contemporaneously, with no product, batch, or data-integrity impact.

intakeReviews.notes: Reviewed as an isolated, contemporaneously corrected documentation entry (single-line correction per SOP-DOC-002) with no product, batch, or data-integrity impact — the cleaning was performed and independently verified on the correct line. This is a documented correction, not a quality event, so a full 8D CAPA is disproportionate to the risk. Recommend closing as a logged correction with no CAPA and trending the logbook for any repeat transcription errors.

Concur with QA Compliance. Root cause is a one-off transcription slip self-corrected within the shift; there is no recurrence trend for this logbook over the past 12 months. Initiating a CAPA would not be proportionate to the risk. Approved to reject CAPA initiation — record retained for periodic logbook trending only.
